topics.title: Inspection Mindset
topics.body: A good inspection is organized, visual, and defensible. Work from broad site conditions toward individual systems, separate facts from opinions, and write comments that explain implication and recommended action.
checklist: Confirm client scope before the walkthrough.
Photograph conditions before touching or moving anything.
Use consistent direction labels: front, rear, left, and right from the street.
topics.reportLanguage: The inspection was visual and limited to readily accessible components at the time of inspection. Further evaluation by a qualified contractor is recommended where hidden conditions or specialized testing may be required.
topics.examTip: Inspection standards describe what must be observed, not a guarantee that every concealed defect will be found.
topics.fieldNote: Keep your photo sequence in the same order as your report sections. It saves time and reduces missed comments.
figures: Inspector field attire and site-safety awareness.

topics.title: Drainage and Grading
topics.body: Site drainage should move water away from the structure. Watch for negative slope, settled backfill, blocked drains, high soil at siding, and evidence of ponding near foundations.
checklist: Look for a visible slope away from foundation walls.
Check downspout discharge locations.
Note hardscape that traps water against the structure.
topics.reportLanguage: Grading appears to slope toward the foundation in one or more areas. Correct the grade to direct water away from the building and reduce moisture risk.
topics.examTip: Poor grading can lead to foundation moisture, wood decay, and settlement indicators.
topics.fieldNote: Take one wide context photo before close-ups so the condition can be located on the property.
figures: Vegetation and grading conditions near the structure.

topics.title: Wall Coverings and Trim
topics.body: Exterior wall systems should shed water, resist decay, and remain properly clear of soil and roof surfaces. Inspect joints, flashing transitions, penetrations, paint failure, and material-specific distress.
checklist: Check siding clearance at soil and roof lines.
Inspect trim ends and lower corners for decay.
Look for open penetrations around utilities.
topics.reportLanguage: Exterior trim shows deterioration consistent with moisture exposure. Repair or replacement by a qualified contractor is recommended.
topics.examTip: Know the difference between cosmetic weathering and conditions that can allow water entry.
topics.fieldNote: When one failed caulk joint is visible, scan nearby similar joints for a pattern.

topics.title: Roof Coverings and Flashing
topics.body: Roof inspections focus on water-shedding performance. Document damaged coverings, improper flashing, missing kickout flashing, exposed fasteners, debris, ponding, and aging indicators.
checklist: Identify roof-covering type and approximate condition.
Inspect valleys, sidewalls, chimneys, and pipe penetrations.
Check gutters and discharge for blockage or overflow clues.
topics.reportLanguage: Roof covering damage was observed. Repair by a qualified roofing contractor is recommended to reduce the risk of leakage.
topics.examTip: Flashing defects are often more important than the roof surface because transitions are common leak points.
topics.fieldNote: If a roof cannot be safely walked, state the inspection method used.
figures: Roof inspection from a ladder edge.

topics.title: Moisture and Ventilation
topics.body: Attics reveal roof leaks, condensation, blocked ventilation, poor insulation, and unsafe exhaust routing. Look for staining, microbial-like growth, compressed insulation, and bath fans ending in the attic.
checklist: Confirm bathroom and dryer exhausts terminate outdoors.
Look for daylight at roof penetrations and eaves.
Estimate insulation depth where visible.
topics.reportLanguage: Ventilation or exhaust conditions in the attic may contribute to elevated moisture. Correction by a qualified contractor is recommended.
topics.examTip: Ventilation questions connect intake, exhaust, insulation placement, and condensation.
topics.fieldNote: Describe visible growth without identifying it and recommend evaluation when needed.

topics.title: Movement and Moisture
topics.body: Inspect foundations for cracking, displacement, bowing, settlement indicators, efflorescence, staining, and water entry. In crawlspaces, prioritize safety, structure, moisture, and pest evidence.
checklist: Document crack width, direction, and displacement.
Check for moisture stains and efflorescence.
Look for wood-to-soil contact and inadequate clearance.
topics.reportLanguage: Foundation cracking or displacement was observed. Evaluation by a qualified foundation specialist or structural engineer is recommended.
topics.examTip: Horizontal cracks and displacement usually carry higher concern than minor shrinkage cracks.
topics.fieldNote: Place a ruler or reference object next to notable cracks when practical.
figures: Concrete crack and exterior movement clue.

topics.title: Panel Safety
topics.body: Electrical inspection is visual and safety-focused. Identify service size where visible, panel condition, missing covers, double taps, incompatible breakers, open knockouts, overheated conductors, GFCI and AFCI protection, grounding, bonding, and overcurrent concerns.
checklist: Do not remove unsafe or blocked panel covers.
Check for missing dead fronts or open knockouts.
Test accessible GFCI devices where practical.
topics.reportLanguage: Unsafe or improper electrical conditions were observed in the panel. Evaluation and repair by a licensed electrician is recommended.
topics.examTip: Safety devices have locations and eras. Know where GFCI protection is commonly expected.
topics.fieldNote: Never reach into a panel. The report is not worth an injury.

topics.title: Water Heaters and Drainage
topics.body: Inspect water heaters for installation, venting, TPR valve discharge, corrosion, leaks, seismic restraint where applicable, and age indicators. At fixtures, run water long enough to observe drainage and leaks.
checklist: Locate the main water shutoff if visible.
Check TPR discharge pipe termination.
Look below sinks after operating fixtures.
topics.reportLanguage: The water-heater temperature-pressure relief discharge pipe is missing, improper, or not safely terminated. Correction by a qualified plumber is recommended.
topics.examTip: TPR valves protect against excessive temperature and pressure; the discharge pipe must route safely.
topics.fieldNote: Run adjacent fixtures together when possible to reveal weak flow or slow drainage under demand.

topics.title: Operation and Safety
topics.body: Operate HVAC systems using normal controls when conditions allow. Document age, visible condition, air-filter condition, condensate management, venting, clearance, and evidence of previous malfunction.
checklist: Avoid operating cooling equipment in unsafe low-temperature conditions.
Check filter access and condition.
Inspect condensate drain routing where visible.
topics.reportLanguage: Heating or cooling equipment showed visible defects or performance concerns. Evaluation by a qualified HVAC contractor is recommended.
topics.examTip: Inspection standards allow operating limitations for safety and weather conditions.
topics.fieldNote: Record thermostat mode and outdoor temperature when cooling operation is limited.

topics.title: Fireplace Conditions
topics.body: Inspect readily visible fireplace and chimney components for cracking, gaps, missing caps, deteriorated mortar, creosote buildup, inadequate hearth extension, and unsafe modifications.
checklist: Look for cracks in firebrick and mortar.
Check damper operation if accessible.
Inspect the chimney exterior from safe vantage points.
topics.reportLanguage: Fireplace or chimney defects were observed. A level-two chimney evaluation by a qualified chimney professional is recommended before use.
topics.examTip: A home inspection is not a full internal chimney scan; recommend specialized evaluation when visible concerns exist.
topics.fieldNote: Ask whether fireplaces are used, but base the report on visible conditions.

topics.title: Interior Surfaces
topics.body: Interior defects often point back to exterior water management, roof leakage, plumbing leaks, or structural movement. Document stains, cracks, uneven floors, loose railings, and unsafe stairs.
checklist: Scan ceilings below bathrooms and roof penetrations.
Check stair handrails and guardrails.
Note cracks that align across multiple surfaces.
topics.reportLanguage: Interior staining was observed. The source could not be confirmed during the visual inspection; further evaluation and repair are recommended.
topics.examTip: A stain is evidence of moisture history, not proof of an active leak unless verified.
topics.fieldNote: Use a moisture meter as a screening tool, not as a final diagnosis.

topics.title: Operation and Water Entry
topics.body: Operate a representative number of doors and windows. Look for failed seals, damaged frames, missing locks, safety-glazing concerns, egress limitations, and stains below openings.
checklist: Check representative operation.
Look for fogged insulated glass.
Confirm sleeping-room egress conditions where required by scope.
topics.reportLanguage: One or more windows showed failed seals or impaired operation. Repair or replacement by a qualified contractor is recommended.
topics.examTip: Egress questions focus on sleeping rooms and emergency escape and rescue openings.
topics.fieldNote: Photograph the window and room context for egress-related comments.
figures: Door trim and exterior flashing detail.

topics.title: Wet-Room Checks
topics.body: Kitchens, bathrooms, and laundry areas combine water, electricity, ventilation, and finish materials. Run fixtures, inspect below cabinets, test GFCI where accessible, and verify dryer exhaust routing.
checklist: Run sinks long enough to inspect drain piping.
Check toilet stability and visible leaks.
Inspect dryer vent material and termination where visible.
topics.reportLanguage: Moisture damage or leakage was observed at a wet-area fixture. Repair by a qualified contractor is recommended.
topics.examTip: Dryer vents should terminate outdoors and use appropriate smooth metal ducting where required.
topics.fieldNote: Open vanity doors before and after running water. Fresh drips are easier to spot.

topics.title: Decks and Garages
topics.body: Attached structures create frequent safety and moisture concerns. Inspect guardrails, handrails, ledger attachment, flashing, stair geometry, garage fire separation, vehicle doors, and openers.
checklist: Inspect deck ledger attachment and flashing where visible.
Check guardrail height and looseness.
Test garage auto-reverse features when practical.
topics.reportLanguage: Deck or guardrail defects were observed that may affect safety. Evaluation and repair by a qualified contractor is recommended.
topics.examTip: Deck ledger attachment and guardrail stability are high-value safety topics.
topics.fieldNote: Push gently on guardrails. Movement can reveal what photos alone miss.

topics.title: Screening and Referral
topics.body: Home inspectors generally identify visible suspect conditions and recommend specialized testing rather than confirming environmental hazards. Watch for moisture-related growth, asbestos-like materials, lead-era surfaces, radon risk, fuel-oil tanks, and contaminated soil indicators.
checklist: Describe visible suspect material without certainty.
Note moisture conditions that could support growth.
Recommend appropriate testing or specialist evaluation.
topics.reportLanguage: Suspect material or an environmental concern was observed. Laboratory testing or evaluation by a qualified environmental specialist is recommended.
topics.examTip: Visual observations are not laboratory confirmation; careful wording and referral are essential.
topics.fieldNote: State the visible condition, location, and reason for referral without diagnosing the material.
